44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81

83	<path/to/file.lean>

87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```

92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116

136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.

33

35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

126	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
127	
128	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
129	
130	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
131	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
132	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
133	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
134	
135	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. The body lifts
136	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
137	the small Zariski site of `X` (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
138	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
139	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
140	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
141	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
142	
143	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
144	
145	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
146	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
147	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
148	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
149	
150	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. The body inherits
151	the morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
152	sheafification (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
153	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
154	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
155	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
156	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
157	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
158	
159	/-- **`⊗`-invertibility of an `𝒪_X`-module.** (Blueprint
160	`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`.) `M : X.Modules` is `⊗`-invertible when it
161	admits a tensor inverse: an object `N` with `M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`, where
162	`𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the designated unit. This is the
163	scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's `Module.Invertible`; the predicate carries its
164	inverse witness existentially, so the dual needed by the relative Picard group
165	law is definitional. -/
166	def IsInvertible {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
167	  ∃ N : X.Modules, Nonempty (tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
168	
169	/-- **The sheaf-level dual `M^∨ := ℋom_{𝒪_X}(M, 𝒪_X)`** of an `𝒪_X`-module.
170	
171	For a scheme `X` and `M : X.Modules`, the dual `dual M : X.Modules` is the
172	sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules dual `PresheafOfModules.dual` of the
173	underlying presheaf of `M` (the internal hom into the structure presheaf,
174	`M^∨(U) = ℋom_{𝒪_X|_U}(M|_U, 𝒪_X|_U)`).
175	
176	Construction = the **exact dual analogue of `tensorObj`** (this file, `tensorObj`):
177	apply the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 …)` on the
178	small Zariski site of `X` to the (axiom-clean, sub-step-3) presheaf dual
179	`PresheafOfModules.dual M.val`. The scheme's structure presheaf `X.presheaf` is
180	`CommRingCat`-valued over the single-universe topological site `Opens X`, hence is
181	exactly the base `R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}` that `PresheafOfModules.dual`
182	requires (the value `M^∨(U) = M|_U ⟶ R|_U` is an `R(U)`-module, needing
183	commutativity) — no CommRingCat/RingCat re-bridging is needed, since
184	`tensorObj` already takes `(R := X.presheaf)` over the same CommRingCat presheaf
185	and `X.ringCatSheaf.val = X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat` definitionally.
186	
187	The sheafification functor already lands in `SheafOfModules`, so no manual
188	`Presheaf.IsSheaf` / sheaf-condition descent is needed (sheafifying an already-sheaf
189	gives an iso object; this is the file's convention, matching `tensorObj`).
190	
191	Per blueprint `lem:internal_hom_isSheaf` (§`sec:tensorobj_dual_infra`); Stacks
192	tags 01CM (internal hom into a sheaf is a sheaf) / 01CR item 2. This is the
193	`⊗`-inverse candidate of an invertible sheaf, feeding `exists_tensorObj_inverse`. -/
194	noncomputable def dual {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
195	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
196	      (PresheafOfModules.dual (R₀ := X.presheaf) M.val) :
197	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
198	
199	/-- **The sheaf-level dual is contravariantly functorial in isomorphisms.** An isomorphism
200	`e : M ≅ M'` in `X.Modules` induces `dual M' ≅ dual M`, obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level
201	dual iso `PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso` of the underlying presheaf isomorphism. This is the
202	reusable "dual respects isos" ingredient (the dual analogue of `tensorObjIsoOfIso`) feeding the
203	assembly of `dual_isLocallyTrivial`: a trivialisation `L.restrict f ≅ 𝒪` yields, contravariantly,
204	`dual 𝒪 ≅ dual (L.restrict f)`. -/
205	noncomputable def dualIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' : X.Modules} (e : M ≅ M') :
206	    dual M' ≅ dual M :=
207	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
208	    (PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso (R₀ := X.presheaf)
209	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e))
210	
211	/-! ## §2. (Off the critical path) the full monoidal-category structure
212	
213	iter-206 PIVOT: the full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance is
214	**deliberately not built**. Per blueprint `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path`,
215	the relative Picard group law is a structure on *isomorphism classes* of line
216	bundles — every group axiom is a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` proposition, so no monoidal
217	coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) and no `MonoidalClosed` structure is ever
218	consumed. The earlier `monoidalCategory := sorry` instance (and the two
219	localization-transport supplements `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`,
220	`monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`) routed through the verified-absent
221	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` wall; they are removed here. The group
222	law is built directly on the line-bundle subcategory from the four
223	existence-of-iso lemmas below, mirroring Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic`. -/
224	
225	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
226	
227	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
228	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
229	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
230	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
231	
232	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
233	
234	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
235	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
236	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
237	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
238	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
239	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
240	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
241	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
242	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
243	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
244	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
245	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
246	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
247	
248	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
249	sheaf.**
250	
251	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
252	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
253	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
254	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
255	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
256	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
257	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
258	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
259	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
260	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
261	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
262	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
263	
264	/-- **Left unitor for `⊗_X`.** `𝒪_X ⊗_X M ≅ M`. (Blueprint
265	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, left half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor`.)
266	Sheafification of the presheaf-level left unitor `λ_ M.val`, composed with the
267	sheafification counit identifying `sheafification M.val` with the (already-sheaf)
268	`M`. The cheap `mapIso` pattern; uses no abstract pullback. -/
269	noncomputable def tensorObj_left_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
270	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) M ≅ M :=
271	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
272	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).leftUnitor M.val) ≪≫
273	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
274	
275	/-- **Right unitor for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X 𝒪_X ≅ M`. (Blueprint
276	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, right half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor`.)
277	Sheafification of the presheaf-level right unitor `ρ_ M.val`, composed with the
278	sheafification counit. -/
279	noncomputable def tensorObj_right_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :

730	/-! ## §5. The invertibility-carrier Picard group `picCommGroup`
731	
732	This is the by-hand commutative-group law on isomorphism classes of `⊗`-invertible
733	`𝒪_X`-modules (blueprint §`sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier`). Every group axiom is a single
734	existence-of-isomorphism `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` read as an equality of iso-classes; no
735	pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence and no `MonoidalCategory` instance is invoked.
736	The inverse is carried by the membership witness of `IsInvertible` itself. -/
737	
738	/-- **Step 1 — associator on `⊗`-invertible objects** (blueprint
739	`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible`). An immediate specialisation of the now
740	*unconditional* `tensorObj_assoc_iso`; the invertibility hypotheses are not consumed
741	(they match the blueprint statement). -/
742	noncomputable def tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N P : X.Modules}
743	    (_hM : IsInvertible M) (_hN : IsInvertible N) (_hP : IsInvertible P) :
744	    tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P ≅ tensorObj M (tensorObj N P) :=
745	  tensorObj_assoc_iso
746	
747	/-- **Middle-four interchange for `⊗_X`** (helper). For arbitrary `𝒪_X`-modules
748	`A, B, C, D`, there is an isomorphism `(A ⊗ B) ⊗ (C ⊗ D) ≅ (A ⊗ C) ⊗ (B ⊗ D)`,
749	assembled from the unconditional associator and the braiding (no coherence consumed).
750	Used to reassociate the four factors in `IsInvertible.tensorObj`. -/
751	private noncomputable def tensorObj_middleFour {X : Scheme.{u}} (A B C D : X.Modules) :
752	    tensorObj (tensorObj A B) (tensorObj C D)
753	      ≅ tensorObj (tensorObj A C) (tensorObj B D) :=
754	  tensorObj_assoc_iso ≪≫
755	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (Iso.refl A)
756	      (tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm ≪≫
757	        tensorObjIsoOfIso (tensorObj_braiding B C) (Iso.refl D) ≪≫
758	        tensorObj_assoc_iso) ≪≫
759	    tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm
760	
761	/-- **Step 3 — `⊗`-invertibility is closed under tensor product** (blueprint
762	`lem:isinvertible_tensor`). If `M, M'` are `⊗`-invertible with inverses `N, N'`,
763	then `N ⊗ N'` is a tensor inverse of `M ⊗ M'`. -/
764	theorem IsInvertible.tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' : X.Modules}
765	    (hM : IsInvertible M) (hM' : IsInvertible M') :
766	    IsInvertible (Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M M') := by
767	  obtain ⟨N, ⟨e⟩⟩ := hM
768	  obtain ⟨N', ⟨e'⟩⟩ := hM'
769	  exact ⟨Scheme.Modules.tensorObj N N',
770	    ⟨tensorObj_middleFour M M' N N' ≪≫ tensorObjIsoOfIso e e' ≪≫ tensorObj_unit_iso⟩⟩
771	
772	/-- **Step 4 — the structure sheaf is `⊗`-invertible** (blueprint
773	`lem:isinvertible_unit`). Witness `𝒪_X`, iso `tensorObj_unit_iso`. -/
774	theorem isInvertible_unit {X : Scheme.{u}} :
775	    IsInvertible (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :=
776	  ⟨SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf, ⟨tensorObj_unit_iso⟩⟩
777	
778	/-- **Step 5 — the tensor inverse is determined up to isomorphism** (blueprint
779	`lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef`). If `M ⊗ N ≅ 𝒪_X` and `M ⊗ N' ≅ 𝒪_X` then
780	`N ≅ N'`, via the inverse-of-inverse chain. -/
781	theorem IsInvertible.inverse_unique {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N N' : X.Modules}
782	    (e : Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
783	    (e' : Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M N' ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :
784	    Nonempty (N ≅ N') :=
785	  ⟨(tensorObj_right_unitor N).symm ≪≫
786	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (Iso.refl N) e'.symm ≪≫
787	    tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm ≪≫
788	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (tensorObj_braiding N M ≪≫ e) (Iso.refl N') ≪≫
789	    tensorObj_left_unitor N'⟩
790	
791	/-- The setoid of `⊗`-invertible `𝒪_X`-modules: `M ∼ M'` iff there exists an
792	isomorphism `M ≅ M'` (blueprint `def:pic_carrier`). -/
793	instance picSetoid (X : Scheme.{u}) : Setoid {M : X.Modules // IsInvertible M} where
794	  r M M' := Nonempty ((M : X.Modules) ≅ (M' : X.Modules))
795	  iseqv :=
796	    ⟨fun _ => ⟨Iso.refl _⟩, fun ⟨e⟩ => ⟨e.symm⟩, fun ⟨e⟩ ⟨f⟩ => ⟨e ≪≫ f⟩⟩
797	
798	/-- **Step 2 — the invertibility-carrier Picard group** (blueprint
799	`def:pic_carrier`): the quotient of `⊗`-invertible `𝒪_X`-modules by isomorphism. -/
800	def PicGroup (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ := Quotient (picSetoid X)
801	
802	/-- Multiplication on `PicGroup X`: `[M] · [M'] := [M ⊗_X M']`, well-defined on
803	iso-classes by bifunctoriality (`tensorObjIsoOfIso`), landing in `PicGroup` by
804	`IsInvertible.tensorObj`. -/
805	noncomputable def picMul {X : Scheme.{u}} : PicGroup X → PicGroup X → PicGroup X :=
806	  Quotient.lift₂
807	    (fun a b => Quotient.mk _ ⟨tensorObj a.1 b.1, a.2.tensorObj b.2⟩)
808	    (by
809	      rintro ⟨a, ha⟩ ⟨b, hb⟩ ⟨a', ha'⟩ ⟨b', hb'⟩ ⟨ea⟩ ⟨eb⟩
810	      exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObjIsoOfIso ea eb⟩)
811	
812	/-- The inverse class on `PicGroup X`: `[M] ↦ [N]` for the membership witness `N`
813	of `IsInvertible M`, well-defined by `IsInvertible.inverse_unique`. -/
814	noncomputable def picInv {X : Scheme.{u}} : PicGroup X → PicGroup X :=
815	  Quotient.lift
816	    (fun a => Quotient.mk _
817	      ⟨Classical.choose a.2,
818	        a.1, ⟨tensorObj_braiding _ a.1 ≪≫ (Classical.choose_spec a.2).some⟩⟩)
819	    (by
820	      rintro ⟨a, ha⟩ ⟨a', ha'⟩ ⟨ea⟩
821	      refine Quotient.sound ?_
822	      -- both `Classical.choose ha` and `Classical.choose ha'` are tensor inverses of `a`
823	      have h1 : tensorObj a (Classical.choose ha) ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
824	        (Classical.choose_spec ha).some
825	      have h2 : tensorObj a (Classical.choose ha') ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
826	        tensorObjIsoOfIso ea (Iso.refl _) ≪≫ (Classical.choose_spec ha').some
827	      exact IsInvertible.inverse_unique h1 h2)

829	/-- **Step 6 — the invertibility-carrier Picard group is abelian** (blueprint
830	`thm:pic_commgroup`). `[M] · [M'] := [M ⊗_X M']`, `1 := [𝒪_X]`, and `[M]⁻¹` the
831	class of any membership witness of `IsInvertible M`. Each group axiom is a single
832	existence-of-isomorphism: unit laws ← unitors, associativity ← associator,
833	commutativity ← braiding, left inverse ← the witness iso. No monoidal coherence. -/
834	noncomputable instance picCommGroup (X : Scheme.{u}) : CommGroup (PicGroup X) where
835	  mul := picMul
836	  one := Quotient.mk _ ⟨SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf, isInvertible_unit⟩
837	  inv := picInv
838	  mul_assoc := by
839	    rintro a b c
840	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
841	    induction b using Quotient.ind with | _ b => ?_
842	    induction c using Quotient.ind with | _ c => ?_
843	    exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObj_assoc_iso⟩
844	  one_mul := by
845	    rintro a
846	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
847	    exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObj_left_unitor a.1⟩
848	  mul_one := by
849	    rintro a
850	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
851	    exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObj_right_unitor a.1⟩
852	  inv_mul_cancel := by
853	    rintro a
854	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
855	    exact Quotient.sound
856	      ⟨tensorObj_braiding (Classical.choose a.2) a.1 ≪≫ (Classical.choose_spec a.2).some⟩

858	    rintro a b
859	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
860	    induction b using Quotient.ind with | _ b => ?_
861	    exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObj_braiding a.1 b.1⟩

7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140

300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}

377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on

386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}

392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}

456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).

482	\begin{lemma}
483	
484	\leanok
485	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
486	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}

488	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
489	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
490	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
491	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
492	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
493	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
494	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
495	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
496	  % critical path for the tensor group law.

498	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through

500	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
501	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.

503	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
504	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
505	  \[
506	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
507	      \;\;:=\;\;
508	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
509	  \]
510	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
511	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
512	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
513	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
514	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
515	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
516	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.

520	
521	
522	\leanok
523	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
524	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
525	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
526	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
527	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
528	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
529	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
530	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
531	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
532	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
533	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).

536	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
537	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a

541	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of

553	\begin{lemma}
554	
555	
556	\leanok
557	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
558	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
560	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
561	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
562	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
563	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
564	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
565	  morphism
566	  \[
567	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
568	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
569	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
570	  \]
571	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
572	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
573	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
574	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
575	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
576	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
581	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
582	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
583	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
584	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
585	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
586	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
587	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
588	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
589	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
590	  isomorphism}
591	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
592	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
593	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
594	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
595	  therefore extension of scalars along
596	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
597	
598	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
599	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
600	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
601	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
602	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
603	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
604	  the abstract pullback functor.
605	
606	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
607	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
608	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
609	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
610	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
611	   \;\cong\;
612	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
613	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
614	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
615	  comparison
616	  \[
617	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
618	      \;\longrightarrow\;
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
620	      \;\otimes\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
622	  \]
623	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
624	
625	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
626	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
627	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
628	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
629	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
630	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
631	  % "\begin{lemma}
632	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
633	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
634	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
635	  % \begin{enumerate}
636	  % \item The functor
637	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
638	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
639	  % \item The functor
640	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
641	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
642	  %  [\dots]
643	  % \end{enumerate}
644	  % \end{lemma}
645	  %  [\dots]
646	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
647	  % of functors, see
648	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
649	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
650	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
651	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
652	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
653

655	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor

664	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with

676	  \]

915	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
916	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
917	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
918	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
919	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
920	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
921	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
922	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
923	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
924	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
925	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
926	  left-whiskered morphism
927	  \[
928	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
929	  \]
930	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
931	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
932	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
933	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
934	
935	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
936	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
937	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
938	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
939	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
940	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
941	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
942	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
943	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
944	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
945	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
946	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
947	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
948	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
949	\end{lemma}
950	
951	\begin{proof}
952	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
953	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
954	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
955	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
956	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
957	
958	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
959	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
960	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
961	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
962	  \[
963	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
964	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
965	  \]
966	
967	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
968	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
969	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
970	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
971	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
972	
973	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
974	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
975	  linear maps (Mathlib
976	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
977	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
978	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
979	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
980	
981	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
982	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
983	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
984	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
985	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
986	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
987	\end{proof}
